sections.vendors: No new factory list was published this week — the last one remains dated 1 April 2026, published on the standard quarterly cadence at asosplc.com. A refresh would ordinarily be due around this point in the cycle, so watch for a July edition in the coming weeks. No changes to the Code of Conduct for Own Brand Manufacturers & Suppliers were identified.
sections.sourcing: No sourcing-country shifts were announced this week; ASOS's supplier base remains anchored in the UK, China, Turkey, Bangladesh and India per its Fashion with Integrity programme. The main supply-chain-adjacent news was logistics, not sourcing: ASOS completed the £48m disposal of its former Atlanta fulfilment centre, assigning the lease to an undisclosed global consumer brand and selling the automation assets to a DHL Group company, closing out its non-core asset disposal programme following May's £67.5m Lichfield centre sale to M&S.
sections.strategy: CEO José Antonio Ramos framed the Atlanta disposal (completed 1 July) as proof of the turnaround strategy — strengthening the balance sheet and simplifying the business after the earlier Lichfield sale and 2025 refinancing/bond repayment. Combined, the two site disposals cut pro forma net debt (ex-leases) to roughly £180m from £295m. No Topshop/Topman/Miss Selfridge strategy news broke within the past 7 days; the most recent Topshop item (a Summer House resort collection) dates to 11 June.
sections.pricing: Routine promotional activity continues — ASOS's ongoing mid-season sale (up to an extra 70% off sitewide) and standard app/student/referral discounts remained in effect, with no structural pricing changes announced this week.
sections.gossip: Deutsche Bank and other analysts had flagged Atlanta as the next likely disposal after Lichfield, and the completed sale prompted renewed price-target upgrades (Deutsche Bank to 400p from 375p); shares rose on the news, consistent with the market reading it as evidence of balance-sheet repair.
soWhat: No factory list update this week — but one is due on the quarterly cycle, so expect a July refresh; check back for tier changes across China, Turkey, Bangladesh and India facilities.

The debt-reduction programme (Atlanta + Lichfield disposals, ~£180m net debt) signals ASOS has more balance-sheet room to invest in sourcing/product next FY — a mild positive signal for supplier payment terms and order volumes.
With no sourcing or sustainability news this week, suppliers should treat this as a quiet period to get ahead on compliance documentation before the next factory list and FWI updates land.
